title: China
subtitle: 60 days · Beijing to Yunnan
body:
Huaxia Cultural Sphere Pt.1: China
2 months and 1,563 Miles…

China was equal parts bewildering and frustrating. It has been the only place where I've been mocked and felt dehumanized while also being mesmerized by history, infrastructure, and societal norms.

Infrastructure:

Chinese infrastructure has both harmony and dissonance.

Development happens at such a pace where you often see housing units or train stations exist seemingly without the market demand to justify it… Yet.

There are roads or bike lanes that lead to nowhere, while each major city has some of the most robust protected bike network I've ever seen.

In China, a country with the land size comparable to the US, you do NOT need a car to survive. Infrastructure development has been so fast and so robust — you can take high speed rail from one province to another, arrive and hop on the local metro to your neighborhood, then use the nationwide bike share system to your destination.

In terms of infrastructure, China is winning… by a lot!

The Human Experience:

Ostensibly, China has over 1.4 billion people… BILLION. The vast majority of them know very little English and have little to no interactions with foreigners.

This results in a shared sense of bewilderment from the locals, especially the farmers, when they see a 6 foot 2 white man with long, blonde hair trekking through their small town or village on a bike.

I was met with unbreakable stares and unsolicited photos taken of me, not with me. I had individuals physically stop me so they could take a photo of me.

These constant interactions began to wear on me over time where it became difficult to relax. I found myself becoming short with others, or trying to minimize my existence when simply attempting to find food.

But listen, I understand — foreign interactions are rare. I try to rationalize this, but rationalization can only go so far when trying to self-soothe a consistent emotional response to being treated like an alien rather than a person.
